claim: Larry Hodges advises that once a player has a strong forehand pendulum, forehand tomahawk, or backhand serve, they should develop the reverse versions to give opponents a variation they aren't used to.
quote: To make these serves more effective you should develop the reverse versions... give you a variation that the opponent isn't used to.

claim: Hodges describes each reversal mechanically: the reverse forehand pendulum contacts the same side going the opposite direction; the reverse tomahawk contacts the opposite side with the racket moving right-to-left instead of left-to-right; the reverse backhand serve contacts the same side moving right-to-left.
quote: For a forehand tomahawk serve, contact the ball with the opposite side, with the racket moving right to left instead of the normal left to right.

claim: Hodges argues direction reversal is disruptive because opponents must guard against both versions simultaneously and many players are stronger against one sidespin direction than the other.
quote: This makes them uncomfortable, plus they now have to guard against these serves as well as your regular versions... some players (including me!) are better against one type of sidespin serve than the other.

claim: Hodges emphasizes the cognitive switching cost: opponents not only have to learn the reverse spin, they have to readjust to the regular serves each time the server alternates.
quote: Not only do opponents have to learn to handle these serves, but they have to now adjust to your regular serves when you go back and forth.

claim: Alois Rosario notes that reading spin depends heavily on prior exposure to the motion, which is why reverse-pendulum sidespin is much harder to read than backhand sidespin moving in the same direction.
quote: I find I can read backhand serves much more easily than the Reverse Pendulum serve because I grew up returning backhand serves but nobody did Reverse serves when I played.

claim: Larry Hodges recommends pairing pendulum and reverse pendulum from a near-identical motion with a low toss, switching to the reverse only at the last second so the receiver cannot commit to a sidespin direction.
quote: You can use this motion and at the last second switch and do a reverse forehand pendulum serve instead... With a lower toss, the ball is traveling more slowly at contact, allowing you more motion at the last second for deception.

claim: TableTennisDaily Academy frames the reverse pendulum motion as the same wrist-back/release and waist-shoulder rotation as a pendulum but inverted, producing the opposite-direction sidespin while the body shape stays familiar.
quote: bring the wrist back to the body and then release it forwards towards the net... rotating the waist and shoulders back and then turning them forward as you contact the ball

claim: Forum discussion confirms the reverse pendulum is most disruptive at amateur and beginner level — advanced players counter the sidespin and the third-ball window closes — meaning club players see the largest payoff from adding it.
quote: this serve is, by definition, to be used against beginners only

claim: Sidespin curves the receiver's return in the opposite direction of the spin, so a misread between pendulum and reverse pendulum sends the ball off the side of the table — the literal 'swing the wrong way' failure mode at club level.
quote: when the ball is hit with sidespin, it will curve in the opposite direction of the spin

claim: Ben Larcombe notes the tomahawk's sidespin-topspin forces the receiver's wrist into a roughly 45-degree angle to return crosscourt, so reversing direction with a reverse tomahawk forces the opposite contortion and explains why the pair is so disorienting.
quote: forces the opponent's wrist into an unnatural near 45-degree angle if they want to return it to the backhand
